{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former rank under the modern shrine ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148== See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Komikado Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former rank under the modern shrine ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former rank under the modern shrine ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Komikado Shrine}} [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former rank under the modern shrine ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former rank under the modern shrine ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Narita, Chiba}} It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Narita, Chiba}} Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{Normdaten}} {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Narita, Chiba}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Narita, Chiba}} {{coord|35.85582|140.35866|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[:en:April 29|April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Narita, Chiba}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[:en:April 29|April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station == Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)] * Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148 == See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]] == External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website] {{shinto-stub}} ==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Narita, Chiba}} {{coord|35.85582|140.35866|type:landmark_region:JP|format=dms|display=title}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} {{Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Narita City]] [[Category:1880s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:1882 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Narita, Chiba]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]] [[Category:Bekkaku Kanpeisha]] [[Category:r]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|250px|Komikado Shrine Haiden]]
Haiden | location=[[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{coord|35|51|22.9|N|140|21|30.6|E|scale:5000}} | deity=Fujiwara no Morosuke (Bunjo-kō) | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[:en:April 29|April 29]] }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]] '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Narita, Chiba|Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[:en:Kazan'in Morokata|Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. ==Overview== {{References|date=October 2019}} '''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]. == Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[:en:Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[:en:Shimousa Province|Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō. In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha. Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety. == Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods. == Gallery == File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11460773]] Wikidata name: Komikado Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:37, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11460773]] Wikidata name: Komikado Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name=Komikado Shrine | image=File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg | location=[[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]], [[Narita City]], [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]] 898 | coordinates={{Coord|35.8558|N|140.359|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity={{ill|Kazan'in Morokata|qid=Q11615294}} | type=Bekko Kanpeisha・Beppyo Shrine | founded=Meiji 15 (1882) | festival=[[:en:April 29|April 29]] | prefecture={{ill|Narita|qid=Q273798}} }}[[File:Komikadojinjya-haiden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|thumb|]]
'''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Narita, Chiba|Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[:en:Kazan'in Morokata|Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==Overview==
{{References|date=October 2019}}
'''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
== Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[:en:Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[:en:Shimousa Province|Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō.
In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha.
Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety.
== Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station </gallery>
== Related Books == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/pid/1112175/1/1 Edited by Yamada Beikichi, "Kinnō Jisei Bekko Kanpeisha Seishi", pages 15-16, Niroku Shinsho, 1935 (National Diet Library Digital Collection)]
* Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148
== See Also == * [[Kenmu Restoration]]
== External Links == * [http://komikado.or.jp/ Komikado Shrine] (official website) * [http://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/index.html Chiba Prefecture website]
{{shinto-stub}}
==See also== *[[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]]
==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20140826161205/http://www.ne.jp/asahi/komikado/jinnjya/ Official website]

== Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods.

'''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, ''Komikado jinja'') is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in [[:en:Narita, Chiba|Narita, Chiba]] Prefecture, Japan. Its main festival is held annually on April 29. It was founded in 1882, and enshrines the [[kami]] of [[:en:Kazan'in Morokata|Kazan'in Morokata]]. It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
==Overview==
{{References|date=October 2019}}
'''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
== Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[:en:Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[:en:Shimousa Province|Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō.
In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha.
Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety.
== Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods.

{{References|date=October 2019}}
'''Komikado Shrine''' (小御門神社, Komikado Jinja) is a Shinto shrine located in [[Nagoya (Narita)|Nagoya]], [[Narita City]], [[:en:Chiba Prefecture|Chiba Prefecture]]. Its former Rank under the modern shrine Ranking system was [[Bekko Kanpeisha]] (special government shrine). It is one of the [[List of the Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration|Fifteen Shrines of the Kenmu Restoration]].
== Overview == The shrine is dedicated to [[:en:Fujiwara no Morosuke|Fujiwara no Morosuke]], a close retainer of Emperor [[:en:Go-Daigo|Go-Daigo]]. In Genkō 1 (1331), Morosuke was captured at Mount Hiei while attempting to overthrow the shogunate as Emperor Go-Daigo's proxy during the [[:en:Genkō War|Genkō War]]. He was exiled to [[:en:Shimousa Province|Shimousa Province]] where he died three months later at the age of 32. During the [[Kenmu Restoration]], he was posthumously appointed as [[:en:Daijō Daijin|Daijō Daijin]] and given the posthumous name of Bunjo-kō.
In Meiji 10 (1877), the local residents initiated a movement to establish a shrine dedicated to Morosuke. In Meiji 12 (1879), permission was granted for the shrine's establishment, and the shrine's name was decided as "Komikado Shrine." In Meiji 15 (1882), the shrine was constructed on the site of Morosuke's tomb, and the enshrinement ceremony was held on April 29 of that year. On June 14, 1882, the shrine was designated as a Bekko Kanpeisha.
Because Morosuke sacrificed himself for Emperor Go-Daigo, the shrine is known as the "substitute deity" and is believed to provide protection for traffic and aviation safety.
== Cultural Properties == * [https://www.pref.chiba.lg.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai/p431-054.html Natural Monument] designated by Chiba Prefecture ** The Forest of Komikado Shrine: This forest consists mainly of planted trees, including Shira-kashi, Camphor, Sugi, Kuro-matsu, and Momi, which were planted around the time of the shrine's construction in the Meiji period. After about 100 years, these trees have grown into large trees, with trunks over 1.5 meters in circumference and heights over 15 meters, creating a solemn appearance. The forest can be divided into Shira-kashi forest, Tabu-no-ki forest, Sugi forest, and mixed forest. The Tabu-no-ki forest behind the shrine has been protected for a long time, and other species such as Aka-gashi are mixed in. The forest is particularly notable for its Shira-kashi trees, which reach heights of 25 meters. The Shira-kashi forest represents a typical example of evergreen broad-leaved forests in the Hokuso region and is considered valuable as it shows the development of artificial forests with known planting periods.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Komikadojinjya-honden-Narita.Nagoya.jpg|main hall File:Komikadojinjya-syaden-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Overview of Shrine Buildings File:Komikadojinjya-torii-Komikadojinjyanomori-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Torii looking into the Forest of Komikado Shrine File:Komikadojinjya-syagouhyou-torii-Narita-Nagoya.jpg|Shrine name Tablet and Torii File:Komikadojinjya-ootorii-Narita-namegawa.jpg|Great Torii in front of Narita Station </gallery>

* Atsushi Suganuma and Yoshihiko Ueda, Editors, "Shinto Dictionary", Jinja Shinpōsha, 1968, page 30 * Eiji Shirai and Masanori Toki, Editors, "Shrine Dictionary", Tōkyōdō Shuppan, 1979, page 148
